author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm CIX

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Every one who faithfully readeth the Acts of the Apostles, acknowledgeth that this Psalm containeth a prophecy of Christ; for it evidently appeareth that what is here written, “let his days be few, and let another take his office,” is prophesied of Judas, the betrayer of Christ.…For as some things are said which seem peculiarly to apply to the Apostle Peter, and yet are not clear in their meaning, unless when referred to the Church, whom he is acknowledged to have figuratively represented, on account of the primacy which he bore among the Disciples; as it is written, “I will give unto thee the keys of the kingdom of heaven,” and other passages of the like purport: so Judas doth represent those Jews who were enemies of Christ, who both then hated Christ, and now, in their line of succession, this species of wickedness continuing, hate Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Of these men, and of this people, not only may what we read more openly discovered in this Psalm be conveniently understood, but also those things which are more expressly stated concerning Judas himself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Psalm, then, beginneth thus: “O God, be not silent as to my praise; for the mouth of the ungodly, yea, the mouth of the deceitful is opened upon me” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence it appeareth, both that the blame, which the ungodly and the deceitful is not silent of, is false, and that the praise, which God is not silent of, is true.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For God is true, but every man a liar;” for no man is true, except him in whom God speaketh.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But the highest praise is that of the only-begotten Son of God, in which He is proclaimed even that which He is, the only-begotten Son of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But this did not appear, but, when His weakness appeared, lay hid, when the mouth of the ungodly and deceitful was opened upon Him; and for this reason his mouth was opened, because His virtue was concealed: and he saith, “the mouth of the deceitful was opened,” because the hatred which was covered by deceit burst out into language.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They have spoken against me with false tongues” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 2): then chiefly when they praised him as a “good Master” with insidious adulation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence it is elsewhere said: “and they that praised me, are sworn together against me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Next, because they burst into cries, “Crucify Him, crucify Him;” he hath added, “They compassed me about also with words of hatred.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They who with a treacherous tongue spoke words seemingly of love, and not of hatred, “against me,” since they did this insidiously; afterwards “compassed me about with words” not of false and deceitful love, but of open “hatred, and fought against me without a cause.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For as the pious love Christ for nought, so do the wicked hate Him for nought; for as truth is earnestly sought by the best men on its own account, without any advantage, external to itself, in view, so is wickedness sought by the worst men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence among secular authors it is said of a very bad man, “he was wicked and cruel for no object.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “In place,” saith he, “of loving me, they detracted from me” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There are six different acts of this class, which may, when mentioned, very easily be borne in mind; (1) to return good for evil, (2) not to return evil for evil; (3) to return good for good, (4) to return evil for evil; (5) not to return good for good, (6) to return evil for good.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The two first of these belong to the good, and the first of these two is the better; the two last belong to the wicked, and the latter of the two is the worse; the two middle to a sort of middle class of persons, but the first of these borders upon the good, the latter on the bad.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We should remark these things in the holy Scriptures.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Our Lord Himself returneth good for evil, who “justifieth the ungodly;” and who, when hanging upon the Cross, said, “Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But after he had said, “in place of loving me, they detracted from me;” what doth he add?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But I gave myself unto prayer.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He said not indeed what he prayed, but what can we better understand than for them themselves?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they were detracting greatly from Him whom they crucified, when they ridiculed Him as if He were a man, whom in their opinion they had conquered; from which Cross He said, “Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do;” so that while they in the depth of their malignity were rendering evil for good, He in the height of His goodness was rendering good for evil.…The divine words then teach us by our Lord’s example, that when we feel others ungrateful to us, not only in that they do not repay us with good, but even return evil for good, we should pray; He indeed for others who were raging against Him, or in sorrow, or endangered in faith; but we for ourselves in the first place, that we may by the mercy and aid of God conquer our own mind, by which we are borne on to the desire of revenge, when any detraction is made from us, either in our presence or our absence.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He addeth, “Thus have they rewarded me evil for good” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And as if we asked, what evil? for what good?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And hatred,” he saith, “for my good will.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is the sum total of their great guilt.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For how could the persecutors injure Him who died of His own free-will, and not by compulsion?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But this very hatred is the greatest crime of the persecutor, although it be the willing atonement of the sufferer.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And he hath sufficiently explained the sense of the above words, “In place of loving me,” since they owed love not as a general duty only, but in return for His love: in that he hath here added, “for my good will.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This love He mentioneth in the Gospel, when He saith, “How often would I have gathered thy children together, and thou wouldest not!”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He then beginneth to prophesy what they should receive for this very impiety; detailing their lot in such a manner, as if he wished its realization from a desire of revenge.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Some not understanding this mode of predicting the future, under the appearance of wishing evil, suppose hatred to be returned for hatred, and an evil will for an evil will, since in truth it belongeth to few to distinguish, in what way the punishment of the wicked pleaseth the accuser, who longeth to satiate his enmity; and in how widely different a way it pleaseth the judge, who with a righteous mind punisheth sins.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the former returneth evil for evil: but the judge when he punisheth doth not return evil for evil, since he returneth justice to the unjust; and what is just, is surely good.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He therefore punisheth not from delight in another’s misery, which is evil for evil: but from love of justice, which is good for evil.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Set thou an ungodly man to be ruler over him; and let Satan stand at his right hand” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Though the complaint had been before concerning many, the Psalm is now speaking of one.…Since therefore he is here speaking of the traitor Judas, who, according to the Scripture in the Acts of the Apostles, was to be punished with the penalty due to him, what meaneth, “set thou an ungodly man over him,” save him whom in the next verse he mentioneth by name, when he saith, “and let Satan stand at his right hand”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He therefore who refused to be subject unto Christ, deserved this, that he should have the devil set over him, that is, that he should be subject unto the devil.…For this reason also it is said of those who, preferring the pleasures of this world to God, styled the people blessed who have such and such things, “their right hand is a right hand of iniquity.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “When sentence is given upon him, let him be condemned, and let his prayer be turned into sin” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For prayer is not righteous except through Christ, whom he sold in his atrocious sin: but the prayer which is not made through Christ, not only cannot blot out sin, but is itself turned into sin.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But it may be inquired on what occasion Judas could have so prayed, that his prayer was turned into sin.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I suppose that before he betrayed the Lord, while he was thinking of betraying Him; for he could no longer pray through Christ.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For after he betrayed Him, and repented of it, if he prayed through Christ, he would ask for pardon; if he asked for pardon, he would have hope; if he had hope, he would hope for mercy; if he hoped for mercy, he would not have hanged himself in despair.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let his days be few” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By “his days,” he meant the days of his apostleship, which were few; since before the Passion of our Lord, they were ended by his crime and death.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And as if it were asked, What then shall become of that most sacred number twelve, within which our Lord willed, not without a meaning, to limit His twelve first Apostles? he at once addeth, “and let another take his office.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As much as to say, let both himself be punished according to his desert, and let his number be filled up.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let his children be fatherless, and his wife a widow” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: After his death, both his children were fatherless, and his wife a widow.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let his children be vagabonds, and be carried away, and beg their bread” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By “vagabonds” he meaneth, uncertain whither to go, destitute of all help.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let them be driven from their habitations.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He here explaineth what he had said above, “Let them be carried away.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How all this happened to his wife and children, the following verses explain.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let the extortioner search out all his substance, and let the strangers spoil his labour” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let there be no man to help him” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 11): that is, to guard his posterity; wherefore followeth, “nor to have compassion on his fatherless children”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But as even orphans may, without one to help them, and without a guardian, nevertheless increase amid trouble and want, and preserve their race by descent; he next saith, “Let his posterity be destroyed; and in the next generation let his name be clean put out” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 12): that is, let what hath been generated by him generate no more, and quickly pass away.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what is it that he next addeth?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let the wickedness of his fathers be had in remembrance in the sight of the Lord, and let not the sin of his mother be done away” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it to be understood, that even the sins of his fathers shall be visited upon him?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For upon him they are not visited, who hath been changed in Christ, and hath ceased to be the child of the wicked, by not having imitated their conduct. …And to these words, “I will visit the sins of the fathers upon the children,” is added, “who hate Me;” that is, hate Me as their fathers hated Me: so that as the effect of imitating the good is that even their own sins are blotted out, so the imitation of the wicked causeth men to suffer not their own deservings only, but those also of those whom they have imitated.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let them alway be against the Lord” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Against the Lord,” meaneth in the Lord’s sight: for other translators have rendered this line, “let them be always in the sight of the Lord;” while others have rendered it, “let them be before the Lord alway;” as it is elsewhere said, “Thou hast set our misdeeds in Thy sight.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By “alway,” he meaneth that this great crime should be without pardon, both here, and in a future life.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let the memorial of them perish from off the earth:” that is, of his father and of his mother.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By memorial of them, he meaneth, that which is preserved by successive generations: this he prophesied should perish from the earth, because both Judas himself, and his sons, who were the memorial of his father and mother, without any succeeding offspring, as it is said above, were consumed in the short space of one generation.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And that, because he remembered not to act mercifully” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 15); either Judas, or the people itself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But “remembered not” is better understood of the people: for if they slew Christ, they might well remember the deed in penitence, and act mercifully towards His members, whom they most perseveringly persecuted.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this reason he saith, “but persecuted the poor man and the beggar” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It may indeed be understood of Judas; for the Lord did not disdain to become poor, when He was rich, that we might be enriched by His poverty.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But how shall I understand the word “beggar,” save perhaps because He said to the Samaritan woman, “Give me to drink,” and on the Cross He said, “I thirst.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But as to what followeth, I do not see how it can be understood of our Head Himself, that is, the Saviour of His own body, whom Judas persecuted.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For after saying, “He persecuted the poor man and the beggar:” he addeth, “and to slay,” that is, “that he might slay Him,” for some have so rendered it, “Him that was pricked at the heart.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This expression is not commonly used except of the stings of past sins in the sorrows of penitence; as it is said of those who, when they had heard the Apostles after our Lord’s ascension, were “pricked in heart,” even they who had slain the Lord.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Psalm then continueth: “His delight was in cursing, and it shall happen to him” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although Judas loved cursing, both in stealing from the money bag, and selling and betraying the Lord: nevertheless, that people more openly loved cursing, when they said, “His blood be on us, and on our children.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He loved not blessing, therefore it shall be far from him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Such was Judas indeed, since he loved not Christ, in whom is everlasting blessing; but the Jewish people still more decidedly refused blessing, unto whom he who had been enlightened by the Lord said, “Will ye also be His disciples?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He clothed himself with cursing, like as with a raiment:” either Judas, or that people.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And it came into his bowels like water.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Both without, then, and within; without, like a garment; within, like water: since he hath come before the judgment-seat of Him “who hath power to destroy both body and soul in hell;” the body without, the soul within.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And like oil into his bones.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He showeth that he worketh evil with delight, and storeth up cursing for himself, that is, everlasting punishment; for blessing is eternal life.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For at present evil deeds are his delight, flowing like water into his bowels, like oil into his bones; but it is styled cursing, because God hath appointed torments for such men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let it be unto him as the cloak which covereth him” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Since he hath before spoken of the cloak, why doth he repeat it?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When he said, “He clothed himself with cursing as with a raiment;” doth the raiment with which he is “covered” differ from that with which he is “clothed”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For every man is clothed with his tunic, covered with his cloak; and what is this, save boasting in iniquity, even in the sight of men? “and as the girdle,” he saith, “that he is alway girded withal.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Men are girded chiefly that they may be better fit for toil, that they may not be hindered by the folds of their dress.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He therefore girdeth himself with curses, who designeth an evil which he hath carefully contrived, not on a sudden impulse, and who learneth in such a manner to do evil, that he is always ready to commit it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “This is the work of them that slander me before the Lord” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He said not, “their reward,” but, “their work:” for it is clear that by the clothing, covering, water, oil, and girdle, he was describing the very works by which eternal curses are procured.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is not then one Judas, but many, of whom it is said, “This is the work of them that slander me before the Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although indeed the plural number might have been put for the singular; even as, when Herod died, it was said by the Angel, “They are dead which sought the young Child’s life.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But who slander Christ more before the Lord, than they who slander the very words of the Lord, by declaring that it is not He whom the Law of the Lord and His Prophets announced beforehand?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And of those that speak evil against my soul:” by denying that He, when He had willed, could have arisen: though He saith, “I have power to lay down My life, and I have power to take it again.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But work Thou with me, O Lord God” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Some have thought “mercifully” should be understood, some have actually added it; but the best copies have the words thus: “But work Thou with me, O Lord God, for Thy Name’s sake.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence a higher sense should not be passed over, supposing the Son to have thus addressed the Father, “Deal Thou with Me,” since the works of the Father and of the Son are the same.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Where although we understand mercy,—for these words follow, “for sweet is Thy mercy,”—because he said not, “In me,” or, “over me;” or anything of this sort: but, “work Thou with Me;” we rightly understand that the Father and Son together work mercifully towards the vessels of mercy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Work with me,” may also be understood to mean, help me.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We use this expression in our daily language, when we are speaking of anything which is in our favour; “It works with us.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the Father aideth the Son, as far as the Deity aideth Man, on account of His having assumed the “form of a servant,” to which Man, God, and to which “Form of a servant,” the Lord too is Father.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in the “form of God,” the Son needeth not aid, for He is equally all-powerful with the Father, on which account He also is the helper of men.…And because when he had said, “Work Thou with me,” he added, “for Thy Name’s sake,” he hath commended grace.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For without previous deserving works, human nature was raised to such a height, that the whole in one, the Word and Flesh, that is, God and Man, was styled the Only-begotten Son of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this was done that that which had been lost might be sought by Him who had created it, through that which had not been lost; whence the following words, “For Thy mercy is sweet.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “O deliver me, for I am needy and poor” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Need and poverty is that weakness, through which He was crucified.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And my heart is disturbed within me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This alludeth to those words which He spoke when His Passion was drawing near, “My soul is exceeding sorrowful, even unto death.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I go hence like a shadow that declineth” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By this he signified death itself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For as night comes of the shadow’s declining, so death comes of mortal flesh.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And am driven away as the locusts.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This I think would be more suitably understood of His members, that is, of His faithful disciples.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That he might make it much plainer, he preferred writing “locusts” in the plural number: although many may be understood where the singular number is used, as in that passage, “He spake, and the locust came;” but it would have been more obscure.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: His disciples, then, were driven away, that is, were put to flight by persecutors, either the multitude of whom He wished to be signified by the word locusts, or their passing from one place to another.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “My knees are weak through fasting” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We read, that our Lord Christ underwent a fast of forty days: but had fasting so great power over Him, that His knees were weakened?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or is this more suitably understood of His members, that is, of His saints?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And my flesh is changed because of the oil;” because of spiritual grace.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence Christ was so called from the Greek word, chrisma, which signifies unction.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But the flesh was changed through the oil, not for the worse, but for the better, that is, rising from the dishonour of death to the glory of immortality.…His flesh was not yet changed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But whether the Holy Spirit be represented by water through the notion of ablution or irrigation, or by oil through that of exultation and the inflaming of charity; It doth not differ from Itself, because Its types are different.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there is a great difference between the lion and the lamb, and yet Christ is represented by both.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I became also a reproach unto them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 24): through the death of the Cross.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They looked upon Me, and shaked their heads.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Because they beheld His crucifixion, without beholding His resurrection: they saw when His knees were weakened, they saw not when His flesh was changed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Help me, O Lord my God: O save me according to Thy mercy” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This may be referred to the whole, both to the Head and to the body: to the Head, owing to His having taken the form of a servant; to the body, on account of the servants themselves.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For He might even in them have said unto God, “Help Me:” and, “O save Me:” as in them He said unto Paul, “Why persecutest thou Me”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The following words, “according to Thy mercy,” describe grace given gratuitously, not according to the merit of works.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And let them know how that this is Thy Hand, and that Thou, Lord, hast made it” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He said, “Let them know,” of those for whom He even prayed while they were raging; for even those who afterwards believed in Him were among the crowd who shook their heads in mockery of Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But let those who ascribe unto God the shape of the human body, learn in what sense God hath a hand.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let us therefore understand, that the Hand of God meaneth Christ: whence it is elsewhere said, “Unto whom is the arm of the Lord revealed?”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Though they curse, yet bless Thou” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Vain therefore and false is the cursing of the sons of men, that have pleasure in vanity, and seek a lie; but when God blesseth, He doth what He saith.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let them be confounded that rise up against me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For their imagining that they have some power against Me, is the reason that they rise up against Me; but when I shall have been exalted above the heavens, and My glory shall have commenced spreading over the whole earth, they shall be confounded.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But Thy servant shall rejoice:” either on the right hand of the Father, or in His members when they rejoice, both in hope among temptations, and after temptations for evermore.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let my slanderers be clothed with shame” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 28): that is, let it shame them to have slandered me.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But this may also be understood as a blessing, in that they are amended.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And let them cover themselves with their own confusion, as with a double cloak;” for diplois is a double cloak; that is, let them be confounded both within and without: both before God and before men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “As for me, I will confess greatly unto the Lord with my mouth” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 29).…Is He said to “praise among the multitude” because He is with His Church here even unto the end of the world; so that we may understand by “among the multitude,” that He is honoured by this very multitude?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he is said to be in the midst, unto whom the chief honour is paid.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if the heart is, as it were, that which is mid-most of a man, no better construction can be put on this passage than this, I will praise Him in the hearts of many.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For Christ dwelleth through faith in our hearts; and therefore he saith, “with my mouth,” that is, with the mouth of my body, which is the Church.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For He stood at the right hand of the poor” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It was said of Judas, “Let Satan stand at his right hand;” since he chose to increase his riches by selling Christ; but here the Lord stood at the right hand of the poor, that the Lord Himself might be the poor man’s riches.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He stood at the right hand of the poor,” not to multiply the years of a life that one day must end, nor to increase his stores, nor to render him strong in the strength of the body, or secure for a time; “but,” he saith, “to save my soul from the persecutors.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now the soul is rendered safe from the persecutors, if we do not consent to them unto evil; but there is no such consent to them when the Lord standeth at the right hand of the poor, that he may not give way through his very poverty, that is, weakness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This aid was given to the Body of Christ in the case of all the holy Martyrs.
